class_schedule.topic: The Four Types of Analytics and the Business Analytics Mindset
learning_objectives: Distinguish between descriptive, diagnostic, predictive, and prescriptive analytics with a real business example for each
Frame a business question as a data question that can be answered with available or obtainable data
Identify the value chain from raw data to decision and recognize where each analytics type adds value
Recognize common pitfalls: correlation vs causation, survivorship bias, and overfitting to past data
readings: Provost & Fawcett, Data Science for Business (2013), Ch. 1 - Data-Analytic Thinking
Camm, Cochran, Fry, Ohlmann, Business Analytics (5th ed.), Ch. 1 - Introduction to Business Analytics
Harvard Business Review, 'Data Scientist: The Sexiest Job of the 21st Century' (Davenport & Patil, 2012)
Thomas Davenport, 'Competing on Analytics' HBR (2006) - the framework article

lecture_notes.introduction: In 2002, Billy Beane, general manager of the Oakland Athletics baseball team, had the smallest payroll in Major League Baseball and a mandate to win. Conventional baseball wisdom said he needed batting average, speed, and veteran leadership. Instead, working with a young Harvard economics graduate named Paul DePodesta, Beane used statistical analysis to identify players who were undervalued by the market: players with high on-base percentage who did not fit the traditional scout's eye test. The A's won 20 games in a row and made the playoffs, and the book and film Moneyball introduced the world to the idea that data-driven decisions could beat intuition in a domain that had resisted analytics for a century. Twenty years later, every major league team has an analytics department, every retailer runs pricing algorithms, every hospital tracks readmission rates, and every bank scores credit applications with machine learning. But the majority of analytics projects still fail to deliver business value, and a surprising number of 'data-driven' decisions are built on flawed data, flawed reasoning, or the wrong question. This course teaches you to recognize which kind of analytics your problem actually needs, how to collect and prepare the right data, how to visualize and communicate findings, and how to build models that predict the future without fooling yourself about accuracy. We will cover the four types of analytics (descriptive, diagnostic, predictive, prescriptive) from a practitioner standpoint, working through real cases from Target, Netflix, Zillow, Amazon, and others where the analytics either delivered enormous value or failed in instructive ways.
key_concepts: Descriptive analytics: 'what happened?' - sales reports, dashboards, KPIs, time-series of revenue; answers the past with aggregation and visualization
Diagnostic analytics: 'why did it happen?' - drill-downs, cohort analysis, attribution, root-cause analysis; goes beyond description to explain causes
Predictive analytics: 'what will happen?' - forecasting, classification, regression, recommendation engines; uses historical patterns to estimate future outcomes
Prescriptive analytics: 'what should we do?' - optimization, simulation, decision models; goes beyond prediction to recommend actions under constraints
The value chain: raw data -> cleaned data -> analysis -> insight -> decision -> action -> outcome; each step adds or subtracts value

Framing: 'what is our churn rate?' (descriptive) vs 'why are customers churning?' (diagnostic) vs 'which customers will churn next month?' (predictive) vs 'what offer should we make to at-risk customers?' (prescriptive)
Correlation vs causation: two variables may move together without one causing the other; classic example is ice cream sales and drowning (both driven by summer)
Survivorship bias: analyzing only successful cases and missing the lessons from failures; Abraham Wald's WWII bomber analysis
Overfitting: a model that fits historical data perfectly but fails on new data; the most common technical error in business analytics
Davenport's five levels of analytical competitors: analytically impaired, localized analytics, analytical aspirations, analytical companies, analytical competitors
lecture_notes.demonstration: Walk through a real example from Moneyball: the A's bought on-base percentage (OBP) because the market systematically underpriced it. Show the math: OBP correlates with runs scored at approximately r = 0.89, while batting average correlates at about r = 0.81. The difference seems small but produces real wins. Then contrast with a predictive model: given a player's 2024 stats, predict their 2025 on-base percentage using regression. Then a prescriptive question: given a $90M budget, which combination of players maximizes projected wins subject to the payroll constraint? Each question requires a different analytics type and a different level of sophistication.
setup_requirements: Laptop with Python 3.11+ and pandas, numpy, matplotlib, scikit-learn installed
Access to Tableau Public (free) or Power BI Desktop (free) for visualization
Sample dataset: Lahman Baseball Database or MLB stats for Moneyball exercise
Access to course GitHub repo with starter notebooks

review.key: Four Analytics Types Answer Four Questions
review.value: Descriptive answers 'what happened', diagnostic answers 'why', predictive answers 'what will happen', prescriptive answers 'what should we do'. Most organizations stop at descriptive and occasionally dabble in predictive; the prescriptive layer is where optimization delivers the highest ROI

The biggest failures in business analytics come from answering the wrong question precisely. Before any SQL or Python, clarify what decision the analysis will inform and what change in that decision would be material to the business

Storks nest on chimneys in European towns that also have more babies - both are explained by town size, not that storks deliver babies. In business analytics, always ask: is there a confounding variable explaining both my inputs and my outputs?

A model that perfectly explains 100 historical data points is usually useless for predicting the 101st. Always hold out test data, cross-validate, and prefer simpler models when they perform comparably to complex ones

class_schedule.topic: Data Collection, Cleaning, and Exploratory Data Analysis
learning_objectives: Load data from CSV, Excel, and database sources into pandas DataFrames
Identify and handle missing values, duplicates, outliers, and inconsistent formatting
Conduct exploratory data analysis using summary statistics, histograms, and scatter plots
Apply the 80/20 rule of analytics: 80% of time is data cleaning, 20% is modeling
readings: Provost & Fawcett, Data Science for Business, Ch. 2 - Business Problems and Data Science Solutions
McKinney, Python for Data Analysis (3rd ed., 2022), Ch. 6-7 - Data Loading and Cleaning
Tidy Data paper (Wickham, 2014) - foundational data shape framework
Camm et al., Business Analytics (5th ed.), Ch. 2 - Descriptive Statistics

lecture_notes.introduction: If you read blog posts about data science, you might think analysts spend their days building neural networks and tuning XGBoost hyperparameters. The reality is that 70-80% of a working analyst's time is spent getting data into a usable state: finding it, cleaning it, deduplicating it, joining it, fixing inconsistent formats, handling missing values, and dealing with edge cases that were never documented. A 2021 Anaconda survey of 2,300 data scientists confirmed that data preparation and cleaning together consume roughly 45% of work time, and exploration another 20%, while model building is only about 11%. The analysts who deliver value quickly are the ones who have mastered the 'boring' work of data wrangling, not the ones who jump straight to fancy models. This session introduces the tools and discipline of data preparation in Python using pandas, the industry-standard library created by Wes McKinney at AQR Capital in 2008. You will learn to load data from multiple sources, handle the five main data problems (missing, duplicate, outlier, inconsistent, wrong type), and perform exploratory data analysis (EDA) that reveals the structure and quirks of a new dataset before you model anything. We will apply these skills to a real sales dataset from a retailer with 300,000 transactions, 50,000 customers, and all the messiness of real data.
key_concepts: Data sources: CSV, Excel, databases (SQL), APIs (JSON), web scraping; each has different parsing challenges and type inference rules
pandas DataFrame: two-dimensional labeled data structure; columns are Series; the workhorse of Python data analysis
Missing values: represented as NaN in pandas; strategies include drop (losing rows), impute (mean, median, model-based), or indicator variable (is_missing = 1)
Duplicates: exact duplicates removed with df.drop_duplicates(); near-duplicates require fuzzy matching (record linkage)
Outliers: values far from the bulk of the data; may be real extremes or data errors; handle with investigation, winsorization, or robust methods
Inconsistent formatting: 'USA' vs 'U.S.A.' vs 'United States'; requires standardization via regex or lookup tables
Data types: numeric (int, float), string (object), datetime, categorical; wrong types cause silent errors (string sorting of dates)
Summary statistics: mean, median, std, min, max, quartiles via df.describe(); first line of EDA
Visualization: histograms for distributions, boxplots for outliers, scatter plots for relationships, correlation matrices for overview
Tidy data (Hadley Wickham): each variable forms a column, each observation forms a row, each type of unit forms a table - the standard shape for analysis
lecture_notes.demonstration: Load a sample retail dataset with 300,000 rows in pandas. Run df.info() to see column types and non-null counts; discover that 'date' is stored as string and 'customer_id' has 15,000 nulls. Run df.describe() to see numeric summaries; notice that one product has a max price of $99,999 (obvious data entry error). Convert date column to datetime. Impute missing customer IDs as 'guest'. Plot histograms of price and quantity to see distribution shape. Build a correlation matrix to preview which variables move together. Discuss what you have learned about the data in 10 minutes before writing any model code.
setup_requirements: Jupyter notebook environment (JupyterLab or VS Code with Python extension)
Reference card for common pandas operations

review.value: Budget 60-80% of project time for data preparation, not modeling. Analysts who underestimate this consistently miss deadlines and deliver low-quality results. Leadership that demands fast model delivery without budgeting for data work is setting projects up to fail

A dataset in tidy form (one variable per column, one observation per row) works with every pandas function, every SQL query, and every visualization tool. Data in a non-tidy shape requires custom code for every operation. Spending time reshaping to tidy format pays back every subsequent analysis

Always run df.info(), df.describe(), histograms, and a correlation matrix before building any model. This catches data quality problems, reveals unexpected relationships, and tells you which variables are worth modeling

Missing values are not just noise - the pattern of missingness often reveals something important. If high-income customers are missing from your income column, dropping them biases your model. Always investigate WHY values are missing before deciding how to handle them

learning_objectives: Compute and interpret measures of central tendency (mean, median, mode) and dispersion (variance, standard deviation, IQR)
Distinguish when to use the mean vs median based on distribution shape and outliers
Compute and interpret percentiles, quantiles, and boxplots for practical business summaries
Apply descriptive statistics to compare groups (customers by segment, products by category, stores by region)
readings: Camm et al., Business Analytics (5th ed.), Ch. 2 - Descriptive Statistics
Provost & Fawcett, Data Science for Business, Ch. 3 - Introduction to Predictive Modeling (statistics review)
Nassim Taleb, The Black Swan (2007), Ch. 3 - The Speculator and the Prostitute (on Mediocristan vs Extremistan distributions)
Jeffrey Leek, 'The Elements of Data Analytic Style' (2015)

lecture_notes.introduction: If a Silicon Valley bar has 10 software engineers earning $150K and Jeff Bezos walks in, the mean income in the bar becomes $10 billion per person, but the median is still $150K. This is not a joke; it is the single most important lesson in descriptive statistics for business analytics. In any business dataset with skewed distributions (customer spending, product revenue, employee salaries, website session duration), the mean can be dramatically misleading, and relying on the mean instead of the median or a percentile distribution can lead to catastrophic decisions. When Amazon reports 'average customer value,' which Amazon customer is that? The 1 million bulk buyers pulling the mean up, or the 300 million light users the company actually depends on? When a SaaS company reports 'average revenue per user,' does that tell you anything about the median user? This session teaches you to compute the full set of descriptive statistics, understand when each applies, and think skeptically about the summary statistics you encounter in dashboards and decks. We will work through real examples showing how a single summary number can hide completely different underlying distributions, and how to use percentiles and visualizations to communicate the full picture without drowning your audience in data.
key_concepts: Mean (average): sum divided by count; sensitive to outliers; appropriate for symmetric distributions
Median: middle value when data is sorted; robust to outliers; appropriate for skewed distributions (income, house prices, session duration)
Mode: most frequent value; useful for categorical data and discrete distributions
Variance: average squared deviation from mean; same units squared; rarely interpretable directly
Standard deviation: square root of variance; same units as data; the standard measure of spread
Interquartile range (IQR): 75th percentile minus 25th percentile; robust measure of spread; used in boxplot whiskers
Percentiles and quantiles: value below which a given percentage of observations fall; 25th, 50th (median), 75th, 90th, 95th, 99th percentiles are standard
Skewness: asymmetry of a distribution; positive skew (long right tail) common for income, product sales, and session duration
Kurtosis: tailedness; leptokurtic (fat tails) vs platykurtic (thin tails); matters for risk analytics
Group comparisons: compare descriptive stats across segments using groupby() in pandas; reveals where differences live
lecture_notes.demonstration: Load a real customer transaction dataset. Compute mean and median order value by customer. Show that mean is $82 but median is $38 - a common pattern where a few whales pull the mean far above the typical customer. Build a boxplot of order values by customer segment (new, returning, VIP). Show that the VIP segment has much higher variance, and the 95th percentile of VIP orders is $400+ while the median new-customer order is $28. Demonstrate how a product manager could use these stats to set reasonable shipping thresholds: if free shipping at $50 covers the 75th percentile of new customers but only the 30th percentile of VIPs, the policy is poorly targeted.
setup_requirements: Jupyter notebook with EDA starter code

review.value: Use the mean only when the distribution is roughly symmetric. Use the median for skewed data (income, prices, durations). Most business data is skewed, so the median is more often the right default than the mean
A single summary statistic hides as much as it reveals. Always report the median, IQR, and the 90th/95th percentile together for a skewed distribution. Or better, show a histogram or boxplot

Descriptive statistics become diagnostic when you compare groups. Mean order value across all customers is flat; mean order value by segment shows that new customers are 40% below existing customers. This is where insight lives

review.key: Outliers Are Either Errors or Signal
review.value: An outlier is either a data entry mistake, a real edge case, or a rare but important event. Never silently discard outliers; investigate them. Sometimes the outlier is the story

learning_objectives: Apply Tufte's principles: data-ink ratio, chartjunk, small multiples, and truthful scaling
Choose the right chart type for the data type and the question (bar, line, scatter, histogram, boxplot, heatmap)
Avoid common visualization errors: truncated axes, misleading pie charts, 3D distortion, and excessive color
Build effective charts in Python (matplotlib, seaborn) and in Tableau for dashboards
readings: Tufte, The Visual Display of Quantitative Information (2nd ed., 2001), Ch. 2-4
Stephen Few, Show Me the Numbers (2nd ed., 2012), Ch. 1-4
Cole Nussbaumer Knaflic, Storytelling with Data (2015), Ch. 1-3
Few, 'Tapping the Power of Visual Perception' (2004) white paper

lecture_notes.introduction: In 1986, the space shuttle Challenger exploded 73 seconds after launch, killing seven astronauts. The engineers at Morton Thiokol had data showing that the O-rings sealing the solid rocket boosters were unreliable at low temperatures, and the night before launch, they argued against the flight in a conference call with NASA management. They lost the argument partly because the data they presented was visualized poorly: it was a scatter plot of O-ring damage against launch temperature, but it only included launches where damage had occurred. The launches with no damage were omitted, so the critical pattern (all high-damage launches were cold) was invisible. Tufte later reanalyzed the data with a better visualization that included every launch and made the temperature dependence obvious. This story, told in Tufte's book Visual Explanations, is the most important lesson in data visualization: how you draw the picture determines what decision gets made, and choices that seem like aesthetics can literally kill people. This session covers the principles of effective data visualization from Tufte, Few, and Knaflic, the practical skill of choosing the right chart type for the question, and the common errors that produce misleading or useless graphs. We will use both matplotlib/seaborn for analytical work and Tableau for dashboard delivery, and we will critique real charts from the Wall Street Journal, FiveThirtyEight, and corporate earnings decks.
key_concepts: Data-ink ratio (Tufte): maximize the fraction of ink devoted to displaying data, not decoration; remove gridlines, borders, 3D effects
Chartjunk: decorative elements that add no information; includes heavy gridlines, unnecessary colors, drop shadows, 3D effects
Small multiples: a grid of small charts showing the same variables across different categories; enables comparison
Truthful scaling: never truncate the y-axis to exaggerate differences; a bar chart starting at 95 instead of 0 turns a 1% difference into a dramatic visual difference

Chart types by question: bar (comparison of discrete categories), line (change over time), scatter (relationship between two variables), histogram (distribution shape), boxplot (distribution comparison), heatmap (two-dimensional relationships)
Color use: limit to 5-7 distinct colors; use sequential colormaps for ordered data, diverging for centered data, categorical only for categories
Pie chart problems: hard to compare angles; use bar charts for more than 3-4 categories
Preattentive attributes (Few): length, position, color, shape - used by the visual system in milliseconds; design dashboards to leverage these
Storytelling (Knaflic): every chart should have a single clear message; use annotations, color emphasis, and title to guide the reader
Dashboard design: one page, key metrics visible, drill-down available, no clutter; the best dashboards answer 3-5 questions, not 50
lecture_notes.demonstration: Recreate Tufte's Challenger analysis in Python. Load the O-ring data (all launches, with damage and temperature). First, draw a bad chart: scatter plot of damage vs temperature with only the damaged launches, showing no clear pattern. Then draw the good chart: scatter plot with all launches, showing damage on y-axis and temperature on x-axis, with the prediction zone at launch-day temperature (31F) shaded. The pattern becomes obvious: all previous cold launches had damage, and the planned launch was far colder than any previous one. Discuss how this single visualization could have changed the decision. Then critique a real earnings chart from a public company's investor deck, identifying truncated axes, misleading comparisons, and chartjunk.
setup_requirements: Challenger O-ring dataset and sample corporate earnings charts

review.value: The default matplotlib and Excel charts have too much ink. Remove gridlines, borders, legends when redundant, 3D effects, and unnecessary colors. Keep removing until the chart starts losing information. The minimalist result is almost always better than the default

review.key: Always Start the Y-Axis at Zero for Bar Charts
review.value: A bar chart is a comparison of lengths, and truncating the y-axis turns a 1% difference into a 50% apparent difference. This is the single most common dishonest chart in business. Line charts can start above zero if the range is narrow and consistent

The question dictates the chart. 'How has revenue changed over time?' is a line chart. 'Which product category sells most?' is a bar chart. 'Is price related to sales?' is a scatter plot. Using the wrong chart wastes the reader's time

A good chart tells the story in the graphic; a bad chart requires the reader to read a paragraph to understand. Use titles that state the finding ('Revenue fell 12% in Q3'), annotations that point at key moments, and color to emphasize the takeaway

learning_objectives: Design a dashboard that supports a specific business role (CEO, marketing manager, operations lead)
Select and define KPIs using the SMART framework and the leading-vs-lagging indicator distinction
Structure a data story following the situation-complication-resolution pattern
Build a functional Tableau dashboard with filters, drill-downs, and formatted visuals
readings: Few, Information Dashboard Design (2nd ed., 2013), Ch. 1-3
Knaflic, Storytelling with Data (2015), Ch. 7 - Lessons in Storytelling
Camm et al., Business Analytics (5th ed.), Ch. 3 - Data Visualization
Tableau whitepaper: 'Visual Analysis Best Practices'

lecture_notes.introduction: A dashboard is not a report. A report is retrospective - it describes what happened during a period. A dashboard is operational - it tells a specific user what to DO next based on current conditions. This distinction, from Stephen Few's Information Dashboard Design, is ignored by most organizations, which build dashboards that display every metric anyone has ever asked for and end up being used by nobody. The Bloomberg Terminal, the most expensive and widely used dashboard in the world, is built for a specific user (a financial trader) doing a specific task (deciding what to buy and sell in the next minute). Every pixel serves that user. By contrast, the typical corporate dashboard has 40 tiles, 30 filters, and zero clear audience, because each stakeholder added their pet metric. This session teaches you to design dashboards the way Bloomberg does: start with the user, define the decision, identify the 3-5 metrics that drive that decision, and ruthlessly remove everything else. You will also learn to structure analytics findings as stories using the situation-complication-resolution pattern, because even the best data means nothing if you cannot make an executive care about it in three minutes. We will build a real dashboard in Tableau for a mid-sized retailer scenario and critique existing dashboards from Fortune 500 companies.
key_concepts: Dashboard definition: a single-view display of the information needed to achieve one or more objectives, consolidated on a single screen so the information can be monitored at a glance
Dashboard types: strategic (C-suite, monthly view, high-level), operational (manager, real-time, actionable), analytical (analyst, deep drill-down)
KPI (Key Performance Indicator): a measurable value that indicates how effectively a business or individual is achieving key objectives
Leading vs lagging indicators: leading indicators predict future outcomes (pipeline value, customer NPS, lead conversion rate); lagging indicators describe past outcomes (revenue, profit, churn rate)
SMART KPIs: Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Time-bound
Common KPI mistakes: too many KPIs (signal gets lost), vanity metrics (big number but no decision), misaligned with strategy, outdated
Small multiples on a dashboard: one metric broken out by product line, region, or time - enables comparison without clutter
Filters and interactivity: drop-downs, date ranges, drill-downs; too much interactivity hides data from users who never click
Color consistency: same color means the same thing everywhere on the dashboard; red usually means attention, green means good
Storytelling: situation (current context), complication (what is going wrong or different), resolution (what should we do about it)
lecture_notes.demonstration: Open Tableau Public and build a marketing dashboard for a retail client. Start with the question: 'Is our marketing working?' Identify 4 KPIs: total revenue, CAC by channel, LTV:CAC, and conversion rate trend. Lay them out in a 2x2 grid with clear titles, consistent color, and a date filter. Add a small multiple showing revenue by channel as a stacked bar. Add a drill-down from channel to individual campaigns. Remove everything else. Then contrast with a bad dashboard example (from an online gallery) showing 30 tiles with no clear story and discuss why it fails.
setup_requirements: Examples of good and bad dashboards for critique
Few's Information Dashboard Design book excerpts

review.value: Before opening Tableau, answer: who will use this dashboard, what decision will they make, and how often? If you cannot answer these in one sentence each, you are not ready to build. Most bad dashboards exist because this question was skipped
A dashboard with 20 metrics tells the user nothing; a dashboard with 4 metrics forces you to choose what actually matters. If everything is important, nothing is

Lagging indicators tell you what has already happened; leading indicators tell you what is about to happen and let you change it. An operational dashboard should emphasize leading metrics (pipeline, queue depth, lead velocity) over lagging metrics (revenue, closed deals)

review.key: Tell a Story, Not a Summary
review.value: The most memorable analytics presentations follow a narrative arc: here is where we are, here is what is different, here is what it means for us. A dashboard alone is not a story; it is evidence for the story you tell in the meeting

class_schedule.topic: Correlation, Simple Linear Regression, and Interpretation
learning_objectives: Compute and interpret Pearson correlation between two numeric variables and recognize its limitations
Build a simple linear regression model and interpret slope, intercept, R-squared, and p-values
Check regression assumptions (linearity, normality of residuals, homoscedasticity, independence)
Apply regression to real business questions and explain the output in plain business language
readings: Camm et al., Business Analytics (5th ed.), Ch. 4 - Linear Regression
Provost & Fawcett, Data Science for Business, Ch. 4 - Fitting a Model to Data
James, Witten, Hastie, Tibshirani, An Introduction to Statistical Learning (2nd ed., 2021), Ch. 3
Anscombe, 'Graphs in Statistical Analysis' (1973) - the famous Anscombe's quartet paper

lecture_notes.introduction: In 1973, statistician Frank Anscombe published four datasets that have identical means, identical variances, identical correlations, and identical regression lines - yet look completely different when plotted. One dataset is a well-behaved linear relationship. One has a nonlinear pattern. One has a single extreme outlier. One has a vertical alignment that suddenly jumps. These four datasets, now called Anscombe's Quartet, are the most important warning about regression in business analytics: the numeric summary can look great while the underlying relationship is completely different from what the model assumes. Always plot your data before fitting a regression, and always plot the residuals after. Linear regression is the workhorse of business analytics: marketing mix models, pricing elasticity, forecasting, credit scoring, and academic economics all lean on variants of regression. Understanding how it works, how to interpret its output in business terms, and where it can fool you is essential. In this session we will build simple linear regression models in Python, interpret slope and intercept as business quantities (how much does an additional $1 of ad spend produce in sales?), and rigorously check assumptions. We will work through a pricing elasticity example: given historical price and quantity data, estimate how much sales change when price changes, and predict the revenue-maximizing price.
key_concepts: Correlation coefficient (Pearson r): ranges from -1 to +1; measures linear relationship only; +1 perfect positive, -1 perfect negative, 0 no linear relationship
Correlation does not imply causation: ice cream sales and drowning both rise in summer; neither causes the other
Simple linear regression: y = B0 + B1*x + error; fits a line to minimize sum of squared residuals
Slope (B1): the change in y for a one-unit change in x; the business interpretation is the 'elasticity' or marginal effect
Intercept (B0): predicted y when x = 0; often not meaningful in business context if x = 0 is outside the data range
R-squared: fraction of variance in y explained by x; ranges 0 to 1; a high R-squared does not mean the model is correct, just that it fits
p-values: probability of seeing the observed relationship if the true slope is zero; p < 0.05 commonly used as 'statistically significant'
Assumptions: linearity (y is a linear function of x), independence (observations are independent), normality (residuals are normally distributed), homoscedasticity (residual variance is constant)
Residual analysis: plot residuals vs fitted values; any pattern indicates a missed structure in the data
Prediction intervals: range of likely values for a new prediction; always wider than the confidence interval because of the unknown individual error
lecture_notes.demonstration: Load a hypothetical retail pricing dataset: 200 weeks of price and unit sales for a single product. Compute Pearson correlation: -0.82 (strong negative). Build a linear regression in Python (sklearn or statsmodels): units = 5000 - 60*price. Interpret: at $0 the model would predict 5,000 units (meaningless); at $80 the model predicts 200 units; each $1 increase in price reduces weekly sales by 60 units. Compute revenue = price * units and find the price that maximizes revenue: around $42. Plot the residuals and notice a slight curvature - the relationship is probably not perfectly linear, which suggests either adding a quadratic term or using a log transformation. Then run Anscombe's quartet to show how four identical-looking summaries hide very different underlying patterns.

review.value: Run a scatter plot before fitting a regression and a residual plot after. Anscombe's quartet shows four datasets with identical regression output and completely different shapes. The numbers alone can fool you; the plots cannot

A slope of -60 is not useful; 'each $1 price increase reduces weekly sales by 60 units, or about $2,400 in revenue at current prices' is useful. Always translate model output into the business decision being informed

review.key: High R-squared Does Not Mean Correct
review.value: A nonsense regression (monthly ice cream sales predicting stock market returns, or spurious correlations from coincidence) can have high R-squared. R-squared measures fit, not correctness. Always check assumptions and domain reasoning

A statistically significant effect (p < 0.05) may be too small to matter for business decisions. A $0.01 sales lift per customer that is statistically significant is not actionable; a $5 lift that is borderline significant might be. Always ask: is this difference big enough to change a decision?

learning_objectives: Extend simple linear regression to multiple predictors and interpret coefficients with 'holding other variables constant'
Handle categorical variables using one-hot encoding and interpret the resulting coefficients
Detect and address multicollinearity using correlation matrices and variance inflation factors (VIF)
Apply feature engineering: polynomial terms, interaction terms, log transformations, and standardization
readings: Camm et al., Business Analytics (5th ed.), Ch. 4 - Multiple Regression
James et al., Introduction to Statistical Learning (2nd ed.), Ch. 3.2-3.3 - Multiple Linear Regression
Gelman & Hill, Data Analysis Using Regression and Multilevel/Hierarchical Models (2007), Ch. 3-4
Kuhn & Johnson, Feature Engineering and Selection (2019), selected chapters

lecture_notes.introduction: Simple linear regression (one predictor) is a useful teaching tool but rarely sufficient in practice. A retailer's sales depend on price, promotion, weather, season, competitor prices, and marketing spend - all simultaneously. Multiple regression lets us model all these effects together, isolating the marginal effect of each predictor while holding the others constant. This 'holding constant' is the most important concept: a multiple regression coefficient tells you the effect of a variable that CANNOT be attributed to the other variables in the model. The coefficient on price is not the raw correlation between price and sales; it is the price effect AFTER accounting for promotion, weather, and everything else in the model. This makes multiple regression both much more powerful and much more subtle. You can easily build a multiple regression in three lines of Python, but understanding what you have built and why the coefficients mean what they mean takes practice. This session covers the mechanics (how to add predictors, handle categorical variables, interpret output), the diagnostics (how to check for multicollinearity and nonlinearity), and the art of feature engineering - the creative process of constructing good predictors from raw data. Well-engineered features often beat fancy algorithms; poorly engineered features can make even the best model useless.
key_concepts: Multiple regression model: y = B0 + B1*x1 + B2*x2 + ... + Bk*xk + error; each coefficient is a partial effect
Partial effect interpretation: Bj = change in y for a one-unit change in xj, holding all other xs constant
One-hot encoding for categorical variables: a K-category variable becomes K-1 binary indicators (one dropped as reference)
Reference category: the dropped category becomes the baseline; coefficients on other categories represent differences from baseline
Multicollinearity: when predictors are highly correlated with each other, coefficients become unstable and hard to interpret
Variance inflation factor (VIF): measure of multicollinearity; VIF > 5 is concerning, > 10 is a problem; drop or combine correlated features
Feature engineering: creating new predictors from raw data - ratios, differences, logs, interactions, polynomials
Polynomial terms: x^2, x^3 to capture nonlinear relationships while still using linear regression mechanics
Interaction terms: x1*x2 to capture effects that depend on the combination of two variables (e.g., price effect depends on season)
Log transformations: log(y) or log(x) when the relationship is multiplicative rather than additive; common for revenue, prices, populations
lecture_notes.demonstration: Load a retail sales dataset with columns: date, price, promotion_flag, weather_temp, day_of_week, revenue. Build a multiple regression: revenue ~ price + promotion_flag + weather_temp + day_of_week (one-hot encoded). Fit in statsmodels and read the coefficients: price coefficient is -250 (each $1 increase reduces daily revenue by $250, holding promotion and weather constant), promotion coefficient is +1500 (promotion days have $1500 more revenue), weather coefficient is +20 per degree F, Saturday coefficient is +800 (relative to Sunday baseline). Compute VIF for each predictor; notice that price and promotion are moderately correlated (VIF = 4.2, acceptable). Add a price * promotion interaction term and observe that price elasticity is stronger during promotion periods. Discuss the business implication: discounting during non-promotion days has less effect than discounting during promotion days.
setup_requirements: Retail sales dataset with multiple predictors

review.value: The coefficient on price in a multiple regression is NOT the simple price-sales correlation. It is the effect of price after removing the effects of everything else in the model. This is both the power of regression and its most commonly misinterpreted feature

When two predictors are highly correlated, the model struggles to attribute effects between them, and coefficients can swing wildly from sample to sample or when a tiny amount of data is added. Check VIF before trusting any multiple regression output

A well-engineered feature (log of income, interaction between season and price, days since last purchase) often improves model performance more than switching from linear regression to XGBoost. Spend time understanding your variables and creating better ones

Revenue, income, and population are naturally skewed right. Taking the log transforms the multiplicative relationships (percent changes) into additive ones, which linear regression handles well. Log transformations are often the single best improvement to a regression fit

class_schedule.topic: Classification: Logistic Regression, Decision Trees, and k-NN
learning_objectives: Distinguish regression (predicting a number) from classification (predicting a class label)
Build and interpret a logistic regression model for a binary outcome (churn, fraud, conversion)
Build and interpret a decision tree model and explain splits in business terms
Evaluate classifier performance using accuracy, precision, recall, F1, and ROC/AUC
readings: Provost & Fawcett, Data Science for Business, Ch. 3 - Introduction to Predictive Modeling, Ch. 4
James et al., Introduction to Statistical Learning (2nd ed.), Ch. 4 - Classification
Camm et al., Business Analytics (5th ed.), Ch. 9 - Data Mining
scikit-learn documentation: Logistic Regression, Decision Trees, KNN classifiers

lecture_notes.introduction: In 2012, The New York Times published a story by Charles Duhigg titled 'How Companies Learn Your Secrets,' which told the story of a Minneapolis father who stormed into a Target store demanding to know why his teenage daughter was being sent baby coupons. A week later, the father called back and apologized: there had been activities in the household he had not been aware of. Target's analytics team, led by statistician Andrew Pole, had built a pregnancy prediction model based on purchase patterns (unscented lotion, cotton balls, large handbags). The model was classification: given a customer's recent purchases, predict probability of pregnancy. The model was accurate enough to identify pregnancies before family members knew. It was also creepy enough that Target had to mask its outputs by mixing baby coupons with lawnmower coupons to make it seem like the pregnancy coupons were random. This case is the perfect illustration of the power and ethics of classification modeling, which is the most common form of business predictive analytics: churn prediction, fraud detection, credit scoring, ad click prediction, email spam filtering. Most business predictive problems reduce to 'given these features, predict this category.' This session covers the three most important classification algorithms (logistic regression, decision trees, k-nearest neighbors), how to evaluate their performance honestly, and the ethical considerations that classification inevitably raises.
key_concepts: Classification vs regression: classification predicts a category (yes/no, spam/ham, churn/retain), regression predicts a number (price, quantity, temperature)
Logistic regression: predicts probability of a class using the logistic function 1/(1+e^-z) where z is a linear combination of features
Log-odds interpretation: a coefficient of 0.5 means each one-unit increase in x raises the log-odds of the positive class by 0.5
Decision trees: splits data recursively on feature thresholds; produces interpretable if-then rules
k-Nearest Neighbors (k-NN): classifies a new point by majority vote of its k closest neighbors; simple but requires all data at prediction time
Confusion matrix: 2x2 table of true/false positives and negatives; the source of every other classification metric
Accuracy: (TP + TN) / total; misleading when classes are imbalanced (99% accuracy on a 99% negative class is trivial)
Precision: TP / (TP + FP); of predicted positives, how many are correct? Important when false positives are costly
Recall (sensitivity): TP / (TP + FN); of actual positives, how many did we catch? Important when false negatives are costly
ROC curve and AUC: ROC plots true positive rate vs false positive rate across thresholds; AUC is the area under the curve (0.5 = random, 1.0 = perfect); good for comparing classifiers
lecture_notes.demonstration: Build a churn prediction model for a SaaS company. Load 10,000 customers with features: monthly spend, tenure, support tickets, last login days ago, plan tier, and a churn label. Fit a logistic regression in Python. Interpret coefficients: tenure coefficient is -0.05 (each additional month of tenure lowers log-odds of churn), support_tickets is +0.3 (each ticket raises churn log-odds - a warning sign). Split data 80/20 train/test. Evaluate: accuracy 92%, precision 85%, recall 60%. Build confusion matrix: we catch 60% of churners but miss 40%. Raise the threshold from 0.5 to 0.3 to prioritize recall: now recall = 80%, precision = 70%. Discuss the threshold choice: if retention offers cost $20 and saving a churner is worth $500, false positives (offering a retention discount to someone who would not churn) are cheap and false negatives are expensive, so lower threshold is better. Then fit a decision tree on the same data, visualize it, and read the business rules: 'if tenure < 3 months AND support_tickets > 2, churn probability = 0.78.'

review.key: Accuracy Is Misleading for Imbalanced Classes
review.value: A fraud detection model with 99.5% accuracy sounds great until you realize 99.5% of transactions are non-fraud, so predicting 'always non-fraud' gets 99.5% accuracy. Always use precision, recall, and F1 for imbalanced classes

The default classification threshold (0.5) is almost never optimal for business. Choose the threshold by comparing the cost of false positives to the cost of false negatives; the optimal is where the marginal cost equals the marginal benefit

Decision trees and logistic regression can be explained to business stakeholders; black-box models (deep nets, large random forests) cannot. In regulated industries (lending, insurance, hiring) interpretability is often legally required

Target's pregnancy model predicted pregnancy without understanding WHY lotion purchases correlated with pregnancy. A predictive model can be accurate without being causal, and using it to design interventions requires extra care

learning_objectives: Distinguish supervised (labeled outcomes) from unsupervised (no labels) learning
Apply k-means clustering to segment customers or products and choose k using the elbow method
Interpret cluster centroids and profile clusters with descriptive statistics
Compare k-means to hierarchical clustering and recognize when each is appropriate
readings: Provost & Fawcett, Data Science for Business, Ch. 6 - Similarity, Neighbors, and Clusters
James et al., Introduction to Statistical Learning (2nd ed.), Ch. 12 - Unsupervised Learning
Camm et al., Business Analytics (5th ed.), Ch. 9 - Data Mining (clustering section)
Harvard Business Review, 'Segmentation and Lifestage' (Weinstein, 2014)

lecture_notes.introduction: Netflix recommends shows, but before recommending, it has to understand what types of viewers exist. Spotify creates Discover Weekly, but first has to group users by taste profile. Airlines price tickets differently for business vs leisure travelers, but first have to identify which is which from purchase patterns. These are all clustering problems: given a collection of customers with many features (purchase history, demographics, behavior), find natural groupings without knowing the groupings in advance. Clustering is unsupervised learning - you have inputs but no labeled outputs to train against. This makes it both more flexible (no labels needed) and less reliable (no ground truth to verify against). The most famous clustering technique, k-means, was developed by Stuart Lloyd at Bell Labs in 1957 but not published until 1982; it is still the most common clustering method in business analytics 65 years later. This session covers k-means and its main alternative, hierarchical clustering, applied to the canonical business problem of customer segmentation. We will build an RFM segmentation (Recency, Frequency, Monetary) for a retailer, identify 5 natural customer clusters, and profile each cluster with business-relevant language ('high-value loyalists,' 'one-time buyers,' 'dormant VIPs'). The goal is not just the algorithm but the judgment about when and how clustering adds business value.
key_concepts: Supervised vs unsupervised: supervised has labeled outcomes to predict (classification, regression); unsupervised has no labels, seeks structure (clustering, dimensionality reduction)
k-means clustering: partition n observations into k clusters where each observation belongs to the cluster with the nearest mean
k-means algorithm: (1) initialize k centroids randomly, (2) assign each point to nearest centroid, (3) update centroids to the mean of assigned points, (4) repeat until stable
Choosing k: elbow method (plot within-cluster sum of squares vs k, pick the 'elbow'), silhouette score, business meaning
Feature scaling: k-means uses Euclidean distance, so variables must be on comparable scales; always standardize (z-score) before clustering
Cluster profiling: after clustering, compute descriptive statistics for each cluster to give them business meaning ('this cluster has high RFM scores, they are VIPs')
Hierarchical clustering: build a tree of clusters by merging closest pairs iteratively; does not require specifying k in advance; visualized as a dendrogram
Agglomerative vs divisive: agglomerative starts with each point as its own cluster and merges; divisive starts with one cluster and splits
Cluster linkage: how to measure distance between clusters (single, complete, average, Ward) - affects cluster shape
RFM segmentation: Recency (days since last purchase), Frequency (orders per period), Monetary (total spend) - the classic retail segmentation framework
lecture_notes.demonstration: Build an RFM segmentation for a retailer with 50,000 customers. Compute R, F, M for each customer from transaction history. Standardize the three columns. Run k-means with k=5. Plot cluster centroids: Cluster 1 has low R, high F, high M - these are active VIPs. Cluster 2 has high R, low F, low M - these are lost one-time buyers. Cluster 3 has medium everything - the middle market. Name each cluster in plain English. Then design a targeted marketing action for each cluster: retain VIPs with early access to products, win back lost one-timers with discount, upsell the middle market with bundles. Discuss how this segmentation is more actionable than a single 'average customer' model.
setup_requirements: Python with scikit-learn, pandas, matplotlib, seaborn
Retail transaction dataset for RFM construction

review.value: k-means uses Euclidean distance, which is dominated by variables with large scales. If one feature is 'income in dollars' (range 0-200,000) and another is 'age in years' (range 18-80), income will dominate the distance. Always standardize (z-score) first

There is no correct number of clusters. The elbow method gives a technical hint, but the best k is the one that produces business-meaningful segments. Too few k misses distinctions; too many k produces clusters too small to act on

Clustering is useless if you cannot explain the clusters in plain business language. Always profile each cluster with descriptive statistics, give it a name, and describe the customers in it before using the clusters for any action

A cluster of 'high-value loyalists' does not mean that loyalty caused the high value - it may be that high-value customers become loyal or that both are explained by a third factor. Clustering describes; it does not explain

learning_objectives: Decompose a time series into trend, seasonality, and residual components
Apply moving averages and exponential smoothing for short-term forecasting
Introduce ARIMA models and their components (p, d, q)
Evaluate forecast accuracy using MAE, MAPE, and RMSE on holdout data
readings: Camm et al., Business Analytics (5th ed.), Ch. 8 - Time Series and Forecasting
Hyndman & Athanasopoulos, Forecasting: Principles and Practice (3rd ed., free online)
Chatfield, The Analysis of Time Series (7th ed.), selected chapters
Amazon Science blog: 'Forecasting at Amazon scale'

lecture_notes.introduction: Every business forecasts. Retailers forecast demand to order inventory. Airlines forecast passenger counts to set fares. Utilities forecast energy usage to plan capacity. Call centers forecast call volume to staff agents. SaaS companies forecast revenue to plan hiring and fundraising. The difference between a good forecast and a bad forecast shows up in warehouses full of unsold inventory or stockouts that send customers to competitors. Rob Hyndman, professor at Monash University and one of the world's leading forecasters, opens his textbook with the observation that 'forecasting is required in many situations: deciding whether to build another power generation plant in the next five years requires forecasts of future demand; scheduling staff in a call center next week requires forecasts of call volumes; stocking an inventory requires forecasts of stock requirements.' The horizons are different, the methods differ, but the core question is the same: given past data, what will happen next? This session covers the fundamentals of time series forecasting in order of increasing sophistication: simple moving averages, exponential smoothing (the workhorse of retail demand forecasting), and ARIMA models. We will also cover the discipline of forecast evaluation - because a forecast you cannot evaluate is a guess dressed up in fancy math.
key_concepts: Time series: a sequence of observations indexed in time order (daily sales, monthly revenue, hourly website traffic)
Components: trend (long-term direction), seasonality (recurring patterns at fixed periods), cyclicity (longer-term ups and downs not at fixed periods), residual (noise)
Decomposition: additive (y = trend + seasonality + residual) or multiplicative (y = trend * seasonality * residual)
Moving average: average of last k observations; smooths noise but lags behind changes
Exponential smoothing: weighted average giving more weight to recent observations; parameter alpha controls responsiveness
Holt-Winters (triple exponential smoothing): handles trend and seasonality in addition to level; standard for retail forecasting
ARIMA (AutoRegressive Integrated Moving Average): p = AR order, d = differencing order, q = MA order; flexible and widely used
Stationarity: a stationary series has constant mean and variance; required for ARIMA; checked with ADF test
Forecast accuracy metrics: MAE (mean absolute error), MAPE (mean absolute percentage error), RMSE (root mean squared error)
Walk-forward validation: train on past data, forecast next period, move forward one step, repeat; simulates real forecasting
lecture_notes.demonstration: Load 3 years of daily sales data for a retail chain. Plot it: visible upward trend, strong weekly seasonality (weekend peaks), December spike (holiday). Decompose the series with statsmodels seasonal_decompose. Fit Holt-Winters with additive trend and weekly seasonality. Forecast the next 30 days. Compute forecast accuracy on the last 30 days of training data (held out): MAPE = 8.5%. Then fit ARIMA(1,1,1) and compare: MAPE = 9.2%. Discuss why Holt-Winters outperformed for this data (strong seasonality). Then show an Amazon-scale forecasting example: they use hierarchical forecasting across millions of SKUs and reconcile forecasts up and down the hierarchy to ensure consistency. This is far more complex than single-series forecasting but uses the same foundations.
setup_requirements: Retail daily sales dataset with 3+ years of history

review.value: A time series plot and decomposition tells you what structure exists (trend, seasonality, outliers) before you choose a model. Jumping straight to ARIMA without looking at the data is a common failure mode

Academic papers focus on complex models, but in industry exponential smoothing and its variants still deliver the best forecast accuracy for most retail and operational forecasting problems. Start simple before reaching for neural networks

Never report in-sample fit as forecast accuracy. Always hold out the last N periods, forecast them from models trained only on earlier data, and report MAPE/MAE on the holdout. Anything else is cheating yourself

A one-step-ahead forecast is usually much more accurate than a 30-step-ahead forecast. Always report accuracy at the horizon that matters for the business decision, not at an artificially short horizon that flatters the model

learning_objectives: Design an A/B test with clear hypothesis, primary metric, and success criterion
Calculate sample size and test duration using power analysis
Interpret test results using p-values, confidence intervals, and practical significance
Identify common A/B testing errors: peeking, multiple testing, novelty effects, and Simpson's paradox
readings: Kohavi, Tang, Xu, Trustworthy Online Controlled Experiments (2020) - the definitive book
Provost & Fawcett, Data Science for Business, Ch. 5 section on evaluating classifiers (overlap with experimentation)
Andrew Gelman, 'The Garden of Forking Paths' (2013) - on multiple testing
Ron Kohavi, 'Trustworthy A/B Testing at Scale' (Microsoft Research paper)

lecture_notes.introduction: Netflix runs thousands of A/B tests per year, testing everything from recommendation algorithms to poster art on each show. Microsoft, Google, Amazon, Airbnb, and Uber similarly run massive experimentation programs, with the largest running 20,000+ experiments per year. Ron Kohavi, who built experimentation platforms at Amazon, Microsoft, and Airbnb, argues that rigorous experimentation is the single most valuable analytics capability a company can build, and yet most companies do it poorly. The most common mistakes include: stopping tests early when results look favorable (peeking), running so many tests that some are significant by chance (multiple testing), confusing statistical significance with practical significance, and ignoring seasonality or novelty effects. Facebook famously published a 2014 paper showing that users shown happier news feed items became more positive in their own posts - an experiment that caused a public outcry about the ethics of manipulation without consent. The ethical and statistical rigor of experimentation is a first-class discipline, and this session will introduce you to the principles. You will learn how to design a test, calculate sample size, interpret results honestly, and avoid the traps that cost companies real money when they make decisions on bad evidence. We will work through real examples from Booking.com, Microsoft, and Netflix experimentation programs.
key_concepts: Hypothesis: null hypothesis (no difference between treatment and control) and alternative hypothesis (difference exists)
Primary metric: the single most important outcome; defined before the test starts
Guardrail metrics: metrics that should not move in a bad direction (revenue when testing UX, performance when testing features)
Sample size calculation: depends on baseline rate, minimum detectable effect, power (typically 80%), and alpha (typically 0.05)
p-value: probability of observing a result as extreme as the actual result if the null is true; p < 0.05 commonly used but frequently misinterpreted
Confidence interval: range of plausible values for the treatment effect; more informative than p-value alone
Peeking: checking test results before the predetermined sample size is reached; inflates false positive rate
Multiple testing: when many tests are run, some are significant by chance; Bonferroni correction or false discovery rate control needed
Simpson's paradox: a trend in groups reverses when groups are combined; always check for heterogeneous treatment effects
Practical significance: a statistically significant result may be too small to matter; always ask if the effect size is actionable
lecture_notes.demonstration: Design an A/B test for an e-commerce site testing a new checkout flow. Hypothesis: the new flow will increase checkout completion from 35% to 37%. Calculate sample size: baseline 35%, MDE 2pp, alpha 0.05, power 80%. Using an online calculator, n = about 9,400 per variant, total 18,800. At 5,000 daily checkout starts, the test runs for 4 days. Run the test, get 38.1% vs 35.2% at day 2 - tempting to call it a win, but peeking inflates false positive rate. Wait the full duration. Final: 37.1% vs 35.0%, p = 0.003, lift = 2.1pp. Compute confidence interval: 95% CI = [0.7pp, 3.5pp]. Discuss: the lower bound of 0.7pp still represents meaningful revenue impact, so the decision is clear. Then discuss a case where p < 0.05 but the CI is [0.01pp, 3.99pp] - statistically significant but the lower bound is negligible, so the win is less certain than the p-value suggests.
setup_requirements: Sample size calculator (Evan Miller or scipy.stats)
A/B test simulation code in Python
Historical test data for re-analysis exercises

review.value: Decide in advance: primary metric, sample size, duration, and success criterion. Then run the test to completion. Peeking inflates false positive rates dramatically and is the single most common cause of bad A/B testing decisions

review.key: p-value Is Not the Effect Size
review.value: A p-value of 0.001 does not mean the effect is large; it means you are confident the effect is not zero. Always look at the point estimate and confidence interval together with the p-value
If you run 20 A/B tests all with alpha 0.05, you expect 1 to be significant by chance even if none have real effects. Use Bonferroni or FDR corrections when running many tests

A 0.1% lift with p < 0.001 may be too small to justify the implementation cost. A 5% lift with p = 0.06 may be worth shipping while running a confirmation test. Statistical significance is a filter, not the decision

class_schedule.topic: Advanced Techniques: Random Forests, Gradient Boosting, and Neural Networks
learning_objectives: Explain how ensemble methods (bagging and boosting) improve on single decision trees
Compare random forests and gradient boosting (XGBoost, LightGBM) in accuracy and interpretability
Introduce neural networks at a conceptual level and explain when they are and are not appropriate
Evaluate the interpretability vs performance trade-off and the role of explainable AI (SHAP, LIME)
readings: Provost & Fawcett, Data Science for Business, Ch. 12 - Other Data Science Tasks and Techniques
James et al., Introduction to Statistical Learning (2nd ed.), Ch. 8-10
Chen & Guestrin, 'XGBoost: A Scalable Tree Boosting System' (KDD 2016)
Molnar, Interpretable Machine Learning (2022, free online)

lecture_notes.introduction: In 2006, Netflix announced a $1 million prize for anyone who could improve its recommendation algorithm by 10%. The competition ran for three years and attracted thousands of teams. The winning entry, announced in 2009, was not a single algorithm but an ensemble of more than 100 different models blended together. This was the moment that ensemble methods went from academic curiosity to industry standard, and today almost every production machine learning system is an ensemble of decision trees - usually random forests or gradient boosting. XGBoost, released in 2014 by Tianqi Chen at the University of Washington, dominated Kaggle competitions for years and is still the default choice for tabular data in many industries. LightGBM, released by Microsoft in 2016, is faster and often as accurate. Neural networks, the other branch of modern machine learning, dominate image, audio, and text tasks but rarely beat gradient boosting on structured tabular data (the kind of data most business analytics works with). This session covers the concepts behind ensemble methods, the practical choice between random forest and gradient boosting, and the role of neural networks in business analytics (rarely, but sometimes, with specific use cases). Critically, we also cover the interpretability problem: a 500-tree gradient boosting model can predict accurately but cannot explain its predictions the way a linear regression can, and regulated industries (lending, hiring, insurance) require explainability.
key_concepts: Ensemble methods: combine predictions from multiple models to get a more accurate single prediction
Bagging: bootstrap aggregating; train multiple models on random subsets of data and average predictions
Random forest: bagging applied to decision trees plus random feature selection at each split; robust and easy to tune
Boosting: train models sequentially where each model focuses on the errors of the previous ones
Gradient boosting: boosting where each tree fits the gradient of a loss function; XGBoost and LightGBM are scalable implementations
Hyperparameters: for tree ensembles, key ones are number of trees, max depth, learning rate, subsampling rate
Overfitting protection: early stopping on a validation set, regularization, limiting tree depth
Neural networks: layered compositions of linear transformations and nonlinear activations; dominate image and text tasks
Feature importance: ensemble methods can report which features contributed most to predictions; rough but useful
SHAP (SHapley Additive exPlanations): explains individual predictions by attributing contribution to each feature; the current standard for explainable ML
lecture_notes.demonstration: Return to the SaaS churn prediction problem from Week 8. Fit a logistic regression (AUC 0.78), a random forest (AUC 0.83), and XGBoost (AUC 0.86). The more complex models are slightly more accurate but harder to interpret. Use SHAP to explain individual predictions: 'this customer was predicted to churn because they had 3 support tickets in the last month (+0.25 to churn probability), short tenure (+0.15), and recently downgraded (+0.10).' Discuss the trade-off: for a CRM system that generates retention offers, the extra accuracy of XGBoost is worth it because SHAP provides per-prediction explanations. For a regulated industry like lending, the less-accurate logistic regression might still be preferred because it is easier to explain and audit.
setup_requirements: Python with scikit-learn, XGBoost, LightGBM, SHAP

review.value: For tabular data, a gradient boosting ensemble nearly always beats a single decision tree or a single linear model. This is why XGBoost dominated Kaggle for years and is still the default for business ML

review.key: Neural Networks Are Overused on Tabular Data
review.value: Neural networks are great for images, audio, and text. They rarely beat gradient boosting on tabular business data and are harder to train, tune, and explain. Resist the temptation to reach for a neural network because it is fashionable

A model you cannot explain to a regulator, an executive, or a customer has hidden costs: compliance risk, trust erosion, and debugging difficulty. Choose the simplest model that meets the accuracy requirement, not the most accurate model possible

review.key: SHAP Helps But Is Not a Panacea
review.value: SHAP values explain individual predictions by attributing contributions to features, but they are an approximation and can still mislead. Use them as a communication tool, not a ground truth about how the model works

class_schedule.topic: Ethics of Data and Analytics: Privacy, Bias, and Fairness
learning_objectives: Analyze the COMPAS recidivism case as a study of algorithmic bias and fairness
Explain sources of bias: data bias, label bias, sampling bias, and measurement bias
Compare fairness definitions: demographic parity, equalized odds, calibration - and why they cannot all be satisfied simultaneously
Apply privacy-preserving techniques: anonymization, differential privacy, and data minimization
readings: Cathy O'Neil, Weapons of Math Destruction (2016), Ch. 1-3
Angwin et al., 'Machine Bias' ProPublica (2016) - the COMPAS expose
Barocas, Hardt, Narayanan, Fairness and Machine Learning (2023, free online), Ch. 2-3
Dwork et al., 'The Algorithmic Foundations of Differential Privacy' (2014)

lecture_notes.introduction: In 2016, ProPublica published an investigation titled 'Machine Bias' showing that COMPAS, a criminal risk assessment tool used by courts across the United States to inform bail and sentencing decisions, was twice as likely to falsely label Black defendants as high-risk compared to White defendants, even when controlling for past crime and other factors. The tool's maker, Northpointe, responded that the tool was calibrated equally for both groups - meaning that when it said a defendant was high risk, the actual reoffense rate was similar for Black and White defendants. Both ProPublica and Northpointe were right. And that is the point. Researchers later proved that several common definitions of fairness cannot be simultaneously satisfied when base rates differ between groups. You can make your model equally calibrated, OR equally balanced in false positive rates, but not both. This mathematical impossibility, known as the 'impossibility of fairness,' means every predictive model used in consequential decisions embeds a value judgment about which type of fairness matters more. And that judgment cannot be delegated to an algorithm - it is a human and social decision. This session covers the ethics of data and analytics: bias in data and models, competing definitions of fairness, privacy-preserving techniques, and the regulatory landscape. You will work through real cases (COMPAS, Amazon's scrapped hiring algorithm, Facebook's Cambridge Analytica incident, healthcare algorithm bias) and develop your own framework for responsible analytics practice.
key_concepts: Sources of bias: data bias (training data reflects historical discrimination), label bias (labels encode biased decisions), sampling bias (subgroups underrepresented), measurement bias (proxies don't capture what they claim)
COMPAS case: calibration equal across groups but false positive rate disparate; the 'impossibility of fairness' theorem (Chouldechova 2017, Kleinberg et al. 2017)
Fairness definitions: demographic parity (equal prediction rates across groups), equalized odds (equal true/false positive rates), calibration (prediction corresponds to true rate within groups)
Amazon hiring model (2018): trained on historical hires, learned to prefer male candidates; scrapped after bias was discovered

Healthcare algorithm bias (Obermeyer et al. 2019): algorithm used to identify high-risk patients used healthcare spending as a proxy for need; systematically under-identified Black patients because they historically received less care
Privacy techniques: anonymization (remove direct identifiers but vulnerable to re-identification), k-anonymity, differential privacy (formal guarantee based on noise addition)
GDPR rights: access, rectification, erasure, portability, objection; applied to any automated decision-making
Model cards and datasheets: documentation standards for ML models and datasets proposed by Mitchell et al. and Gebru et al.
Red teaming: systematically probing a model for failure modes, bias, and misuse before deployment
Informed consent: customers must understand how their data is used and have meaningful opt-out; T&C checkboxes rarely satisfy this standard
lecture_notes.demonstration: Walk through the COMPAS dataset (publicly available) and replicate the ProPublica analysis. Fit a logistic regression on the same features they used. Measure calibration by race: equal. Measure false positive rate by race: Black defendants falsely labeled high-risk at 45%, White at 23%. Both numbers are correct. Discuss: which fairness definition matters more? If the purpose is predictive accuracy for judges, calibration matters. If the purpose is avoiding over-sentencing of Black defendants who would not actually reoffend, equalized odds matters. Neither is 'correct' without a value judgment. Then show the healthcare algorithm bias case: the original paper retrained the model using actual health metrics instead of spending and the bias largely disappeared.
setup_requirements: Case study handouts: Amazon hiring, healthcare algorithm bias, Cambridge Analytica

review.value: Mathematically, multiple competing definitions of fairness cannot all be satisfied. Choosing which to prioritize is a human and social decision, not a technical one. Refusing to make the choice just means the choice is made for you by the default behavior of the algorithm

A model is only as fair as its training data. If historical hiring data reflects discrimination, a model trained on it will perpetuate discrimination. Cleaning this is a data problem and a social problem, not just a modeling problem

The healthcare algorithm used spending as a proxy for need. Spending was lower for Black patients not because they needed less but because they got less - a proxy bias. Always ask: does this proxy actually measure what we think it does, for all groups equally?

The best privacy protection is not collecting sensitive data in the first place. Minimize data collection, anonymize early, use differential privacy for aggregate publication, and limit retention. Retrofitting privacy onto a system built without it is expensive and incomplete

class_schedule.topic: Tool Ecosystem: Excel, SQL, Python, R, Tableau, and When to Use Each
learning_objectives: Compare the analytical strengths and weaknesses of Excel, SQL, Python, R, and Tableau
Choose the right tool for a given task based on data size, reproducibility, and audience
Understand the modern data stack: data warehouse (Snowflake, BigQuery), ETL (dbt, Airflow), BI (Looker, Tableau)
Recognize when to escalate from Excel to SQL to Python as analyses grow in scope
readings: Camm et al., Business Analytics (5th ed.), tool chapters
dbt Labs, 'The Analytics Engineering Guide' (2023, free)
Lauren Balik, 'The Modern Data Stack' (Medium article series)
Tristan Handy, 'Building a Mature Analytics Workflow' (Locally Optimistic blog)

lecture_notes.introduction: The single most common question I get from students starting their first analytics job is: 'should I use Excel or Python or R or Tableau?' The honest answer is: whichever gets the job done most reliably for that specific task. A simple pivot table in Excel often beats a 50-line pandas script for a one-off question from a manager. A SQL query against the data warehouse beats downloading 10 million rows into Excel. A Python script beats a manual process if you will run it more than three times. Tableau beats all of them for an interactive dashboard a stakeholder will browse weekly. The analyst who knows only Python ships more slowly than the analyst who can choose the right tool for each problem. This session is about tool judgment: the strengths and weaknesses of each major analytics tool, when to use each, and the emerging 'modern data stack' that ties them together in mature organizations. We will cover Excel (still the most widely used business analytics tool), SQL (the language of data warehouses and databases), Python and pandas (the research workhorse for most advanced analytics), R (dominant in academic statistics and some industries), and Tableau/PowerBI/Looker for visualization and dashboards. By the end, you will be able to choose confidently and explain your choice.
key_concepts: Excel: ubiquitous, easy, fast for small datasets; fails on reproducibility, version control, and data above ~1 million rows
SQL: the lingua franca of data; fast for aggregation and joins on large datasets; every data analyst should know SQL regardless of other tools
Python with pandas, numpy, scikit-learn: the dominant advanced analytics stack; general-purpose programming, reproducibility, integration with ML
R with tidyverse, dplyr, ggplot2: elegant statistical analysis; dominant in academic statistics and parts of pharma, insurance, and finance
Tableau: industry-standard interactive visualization and dashboards; excellent for stakeholder delivery; less flexible for custom analysis
PowerBI: Microsoft's Tableau competitor, tightly integrated with Excel and Azure; strong for Microsoft-centric enterprises
Looker: SQL-based BI tool now part of Google Cloud; popular with data engineering teams because it promotes modeling in LookML
Data warehouse: Snowflake, BigQuery, Redshift, Databricks; columnar storage optimized for analytical queries at scale
ETL/ELT: Airflow, dbt, Fivetran, Stitch; move and transform data from source systems into the warehouse
The modern data stack: source systems -> Fivetran (ingestion) -> Snowflake (warehouse) -> dbt (transformation) -> Looker (BI) - the dominant architecture 2020+
lecture_notes.demonstration: Walk through the same analysis in four tools: (1) Excel: pivot table of sales by region and product from 10,000 rows; quick and easy for small one-off questions. (2) SQL: same aggregation against a BigQuery table with 100M rows; runs in 3 seconds. (3) Python pandas: same aggregation with custom derived metrics and a regression on top; more flexibility but more code. (4) Tableau: same aggregation as an interactive dashboard that the sales manager can filter; less flexibility but much better for stakeholder delivery. Discuss: Excel wins for quick manual work, SQL wins for large-scale aggregation, Python wins for custom analysis and modeling, Tableau wins for stakeholder delivery. Then show a modern data stack architecture diagram with Fivetran pulling from Salesforce and Stripe into Snowflake, dbt transforming into analytics-ready tables, and Tableau reading from the warehouse.
setup_requirements: Sample dataset accessible in all four tools

review.value: The best analyst is not the one who uses the fanciest tool but the one who chooses the right tool for each task. Excel is fine for small one-offs. SQL is essential for large data. Python is needed for custom analysis. Tableau is for stakeholder delivery

Every analyst should be fluent in SQL regardless of their Python or R skills. Most business data lives in databases, and SQL is almost always the fastest way to extract, filter, and aggregate at scale

A one-click Excel analysis that cannot be reproduced next quarter is worse than a 10-line SQL query that always produces the same result. As analyses become repeated, move from Excel to SQL to notebooks to scheduled jobs

The winning architecture is: Fivetran ingestion, Snowflake or BigQuery warehouse, dbt transformation, Looker or Tableau BI. Knowing this stack and its components is essential for working at any modern data-forward company

class_schedule.topic: Data Storytelling, Career Paths, and Putting It All Together
learning_objectives: Structure a data analysis presentation using the pyramid principle (Minto)
Distinguish analyst, data scientist, and data engineer roles and the skills each requires
Apply the course framework to real business problems from start to finish
Identify the most valuable next steps for continued learning
readings: Barbara Minto, The Pyramid Principle (revised 2009), Ch. 1-3
Knaflic, Storytelling with Data (2015), Ch. 8-10
DJ Patil and Hilary Mason, Data Driven (2015) - short essay book
Stephen Few, 'Data Storytelling' (perceptualedge.com articles)

lecture_notes.introduction: A perfect analysis that fails to influence a decision has zero business value. This is the most important and least taught lesson in analytics education. You can build the most accurate churn prediction model in your company's history, but if you present it in a 40-slide deck with no clear recommendation, the CEO will thank you and change nothing. Meanwhile, the consultant who shows a single chart and says 'churn is driven by response time; we should double our support team for one quarter and measure the effect' gets the decision made. The difference is storytelling: structuring the analysis so that the decision-maker understands the situation, the complication, and the recommended action in the first three sentences. Barbara Minto, a McKinsey consultant in the 1960s, formalized this as the Pyramid Principle: start with the answer (top of the pyramid), then provide supporting arguments, then evidence for each argument. This is the opposite of how analyses are usually written - bottom up from data to conclusion. But it is how executives consume analyses: they want the answer first, and they stop listening if they have to wait. This closing session synthesizes the course: how to tell data stories that move decisions, what analytics career paths look like (analyst, data scientist, data engineer, analytics manager), and what to learn next. We will close with a synthesis exercise: given a real business case, you design the full analytics approach from framing to data collection to modeling to presentation.
key_concepts: Pyramid principle (Minto): top-down structure starting with the answer, then supporting arguments, then evidence
Situation-Complication-Resolution (SCR): the narrative arc that grabs executive attention
Executive summary discipline: a single-page summary that stands alone for a non-expert reader
Data analyst role: SQL, Excel, Tableau, some Python; translates business questions into queries and dashboards
Data scientist role: Python, statistics, ML, experimentation; builds models and does exploratory research
Data engineer role: pipelines, warehouses, dbt, Airflow, Spark; builds the infrastructure analysts use
Analytics engineer role (newer): sits between data engineer and analyst; writes dbt models and maintains analytics-ready data
Soft skills: communication, business sense, curiosity, skepticism - often matter more than technical skills at the margin

Continued learning: books (Kohavi for experimentation, Hyndman for forecasting, James et al. for ML), communities (Locally Optimistic, Data Engineering Weekly), courses (Kaggle for ML, DataCamp for tools)
Portfolio: GitHub notebooks with end-to-end analyses on public datasets; the portable proof of your skills
lecture_notes.demonstration: Walk through a full end-to-end case: a mid-sized SaaS company has rising churn.
